_meta.name: Recovery Frameworks
_meta.description: Frameworks for maintaining change and managing setbacks
_meta.use_when: Content focuses on sustaining progress, preventing relapse, or building ongoing wellness practices

source.creator: G. Alan Marlatt and Judith Gordon
source.publication: Relapse Prevention: Maintenance Strategies in the Treatment of Addictive Behaviors

descriptions.professional: A cognitive behavioural approach to maintaining behaviour change following treatment. The model identifies high risk situations, develops coping strategies, and addresses the abstinence violation effect. Central to the model is the distinction between a lapse (a single slip) and a relapse (a return to previous behaviour patterns).
descriptions.peer_led: Relapse prevention is about staying on track after you have made a change. It means knowing what situations put you at risk, having plans for handling them, and knowing what to do if you slip. A slip does not have to become a fall. The way you think about a lapse can make the difference between getting back on track and giving up.

background.origin: Developed initially for addiction treatment but now applied across many behaviour change contexts.
background.evidence_base: Strong support in addiction treatment. The abstinence violation effect is well documented.
background.why_it_matters: Normalises the challenge of maintaining change. Provides practical strategies. Reframes slips as learning opportunities.
background.limitations: Originally focused on abstinence models. May need adaptation for harm reduction approaches.

key_concept.abstinence_violation_effect: The tendency for guilt and all or nothing thinking after a lapse to lead to full relapse. Antidote: A lapse is a slip, not a fall.

source.creator: Traditional recovery wisdom (12 step programmes)
source.publication: Oral tradition, incorporated into recovery literature

descriptions.professional: A simple self assessment mnemonic identifying four vulnerability states that increase risk of relapse or poor coping: Hungry, Angry, Lonely, and Tired. The model recognises that basic physiological and emotional needs, when unmet, impair self regulation and increase susceptibility to impulsive or self destructive behaviour.
descriptions.peer_led: HALT is a simple check in with yourself. When you feel off, ask: Am I Hungry? Am I Angry? Am I Lonely? Am I Tired? Often when we feel like we are falling apart, it is actually one of these basic needs. Sort out the basics first before making big decisions or tackling big feelings.

background.origin: Emerged from recovery communities. Widely shared in 12 step programmes.
background.evidence_base: Aligned with research on self regulation depletion, emotional eating, sleep deprivation and social isolation.
background.why_it_matters: Addresses fundamental human needs. Easy to remember. Prevents complex explanations when simple solutions exist.
background.limitations: Does not address all vulnerabilities. Some people need expanded versions.

letters.name: Hungry
letters.check: Have I eaten? Is my blood sugar stable?
letters.why: Low blood sugar impairs decision making and emotional regulation.

letters.name: Angry
letters.check: Am I holding onto resentment or frustration?
letters.why: Unexpressed anger builds pressure and drains energy.

letters.name: Lonely
letters.check: Have I connected with anyone? Am I isolating?
letters.why: Isolation increases vulnerability to depression and relapse.

letters.name: Tired
letters.check: Have I slept? Am I rested?
letters.why: Sleep deprivation impairs every aspect of functioning.

structure.how_to_use: When struggling, run through HALT before doing anything else. Often addressing one of these needs shifts everything.

descriptions.professional: A structured self management and recovery system developed by people with lived experience of mental health challenges. WRAP provides a framework for developing personalised wellness strategies across a continuum from daily maintenance to crisis planning. The approach is grounded in recovery principles including hope, personal responsibility, education, self advocacy and support.
descriptions.peer_led: WRAP is a plan you create for yourself to stay well and get through hard times. You figure out what helps you feel good day to day, what your early warning signs are, what to do when things start getting hard, and what you need if you are in crisis. It puts you in charge of your own wellbeing.

background.origin: Developed by Mary Ellen Copeland in collaboration with people experiencing mental health challenges.
background.evidence_base: Research supports WRAP's effectiveness in reducing symptoms, increasing hope and improving quality of life.
background.why_it_matters: Puts the person at the centre of their own recovery. Focuses on wellness, not just illness management. Created by people with lived experience.
background.limitations: Requires engagement and honest self reflection. Works best with facilitator support initially.

sections.name: Wellness Toolbox
sections.description: A collection of things you can do to help yourself feel better

sections.name: Daily Maintenance Plan
sections.description: What you do every day to stay well

sections.name: Triggers
sections.description: Events or situations that might cause distress, with response plans

sections.name: Early Warning Signs
sections.description: Subtle changes that indicate you might be starting to struggle

sections.name: When Things Are Breaking Down
sections.description: More serious symptoms that require immediate action

sections.name: Crisis Plan
sections.description: Instructions for others when you cannot make decisions for yourself

suggested_combinations.how: Use relapse prevention for the overall structure. Use HALT as a daily or moment to moment check in tool.
WRAP provides the comprehensive wellness structure. Relapse prevention adds specific behaviour change maintenance strategies.
